title: Incentives matter, installment #1637
summary_raw:
I had long wondered about this: Performance metrics can misalign individual and organizational incentives. We study a clean case: an NBA player holding the ball as a quarter expires must choose between a low-probability “heave” that can only help his team and protecting his shooting statistics. We model this decision as a metric-driven principal-agent problem […]

The post

summary_ai.digest: NBA players taking end-of-quarter heaves sacrifice team value to protect individual shooting percentages. A study models this as a principal-agent problem where performance metrics misalign player and team incentives.
summary_ai.summary_long: Researchers analyzed a clean incentive-misalignment case: NBA players at quarter-end must choose between low-probability "heave" shots that help their team win and shots that protect their individual shooting statistics. The study frames this as a principal-agent problem where the metric used to evaluate performance (shooting %) creates perverse incentives that conflict with organizational success (team winning). This is a textbook example of how metrics can be gamed when they don't fully capture the outcome that matters. The insight applies broadly to any organization where individual performance measurement diverges from collective goals.
talking_points: When you measure only what's easy to measure, rational actors optimize for the metric, not the mission — the NBA shooting % case is just one visible example.
Principal-agent problems aren't solved by better metrics alone; you need alignment between what the metric rewards and what the organization actually wants.
This frame explains why so many financial services roles (trading, origination, distribution) create perverse incentives that need constant policing.
summary_ai.highlight: A player protecting shooting statistics instead of taking a high-upside team shot at quarter-end is the same mechanism that drives mis-sold products and misaligned risk-taking in finance.

talking_points: Goodness is context-dependent; badness is more universal—meaning risk mitigation strategies (avoiding fraud, illiquidity, leverage spirals) generalize better than 'optimal' product design.

summary_ai.digest: Tyler Cowen argues AI will make it trivially easy to create personalized religions, though most people won't; instead, AI will likely generate niche accretions to existing faiths.

talking_points: AI will make founding a religion as frictionless as starting a podcast—but that doesn't mean people will abandon established faiths; they'll layer micro-denominations on top.
Religious institutions have accumulated capital and network effects; Cowen's real insight is that AI commoditizes the supply side of meaning-making, not that it destroys religion itself.

If true, this is a long-dated secular headwind for institutional religious wealth management and philanthropy—but a near-term business opportunity for fintech platforms serving spiritual micro-communities.

title: Sub-Saharan Africa facts of the day

The post Sub-Saharan Africa facts of the day

summary_ai.digest: Sub-Saharan Africa has quintupled cereal production since the 1960s, but almost entirely through land expansion rather than yield improvement—a model hitting ecological limits as population density rises.
summary_ai.summary_long: The piece examines agricultural productivity in sub-Saharan Africa, noting that while absolute cereal output (maize, rice) has grown nearly fivefold since independence, this expansion masks a productivity stagnation. The gains came from converting more land to cultivation, not from raising yields per hectare. As population density increases and arable land becomes constrained, this extensive-growth model breaks. The implication is that African agriculture faces a productivity crisis unless yields improve dramatically—a structural constraint that affects food security, rural income, and broader economic development trajectories.
talking_points: African cereal output up 5x since 1960s, but driven entirely by land conversion—yields per hectare have stalled.
Population growth is closing the frontier; without yield innovation, the model hits a hard ceiling.
This creates a policy and investment inflection: either African ag modernizes (inputs, genetics, mechanization) or faces food-security pressure and rural poverty lock-in.
summary_ai.highlight: Five times more cereal output in 50 years, yet almost zero yield-per-hectare improvement—a model that cannot scale further.
